lines.text: Ridgeline Academy sprawls at the top of the hill — all glass and brick and kids who already know exactly where they're going.
Okay. New school. New town. Nobody in there knows you cried during the state spelling bee in fourth grade.
Nobody knows you can't do a cartwheel. Nobody knows anything.
Cool. Cool cool cool. Just gonna stand here memorizing the door like it owes me money.
You could be anyone today. That's the terrifying part.
So who are you gonna be?
...Okay. Bell's not gonna ring itself into being less scary. Go.
The hallway is a river of backpacks and noise. Somewhere in it, a voice cuts clean over the rest.
—and that's exactly why I told Coach we need new plays, not new jerseys, my man's out here color-coordinated and still can't—
lines.action: walking backward, talking to friends offscreen

lines.text: He doesn't see the new kid until it's almost too late. Books scatter.
Whoa—!
For one second, the hallway actually stops. A few heads turn, waiting to see what happens next.
Okay, new kid's got main character timing, I respect it. Almost took you out on day one — that's kind of iconic, actually.
So? What do we got?
No, for real — I'm Jordan. People kind of know me around here, so, you know. Good crowd to already be cool with.
Riley. Locked in.
I'll see you around.
A couple of the kids nearby glance over — recalibrating, just slightly, who the new kid might be.
Okay. That... actually went okay?
Yeah. See you around.
Player kneels, gathering the scattered books while the hallway moves on around them.
—anyway, like I was saying—
He doesn't look back. Doesn't need to.
A girl passes — paint under her nails, sketchbook tucked under one arm. She catches player's eye for exactly one second.
...Nice reflexes.
Then she's gone, swallowed by the crowd, like she never stopped walking.
Huh. Okay. Not nothing.
Okay— wait, that's actually good—
Guys, quick's got jokes. I'm calling you Quick now. Non-negotiable.
A couple of his friends laugh along. One boy at the edge of the circle doesn't — just watches, arms crossed, like he's still deciding.
Anyway. Welcome to Ridgeline. Try not to peak on day one, yeah?
That could've gone so many worse ways.
Tray in hand, player stops at the edge of the cafeteria like it's an intersection with no crosswalk.
Jordan's table is already loud, laughing before the punchline even lands.
Near the windows, Maya has her sketchbook propped against her tray, entirely unbothered. A couple kids wave at her from across the room; she waves back without looking up for long.
In between — a scattering of empty seats. Nobody to join. Nobody to leave out.
Two seconds. Maybe three if I walk slow.
Okay. Where do you sit on day one, Riley. Who's that.
I'm used to it. Eating alone, I mean.
Not because I have to — I've got people, art club's got a whole table most days — I just like my own company most days.
But you're not... annoying, so. Sit if you want.
High praise. I'm framing that.
Don't.
A moment passes. Maya taps her pencil against her teeth, deciding something.
...Fine. Look. It's the mascot. As a raccoon that's seen some stuff.
It's absurdly tragic — hollow eyes, a tiny cape, clearly having the worst day of its raccoon life.
Why does he look like he owes someone money.
Everyone owes someone something. That's just, like, the mascot condition.
Okay. That's — yeah. That's a good laugh to start a school with.
Everyone, this is Riley — new, already got jokes, already got hands, basically already one of us.
It's fast. Someone asks where player moved from before they've even sat down. Someone else laughs at something player says before they finish saying it.
Okay, Riley's staying right here tomorrow, non-negotiable, I'm saving the seat myself.
Across the room, Maya glances over once — then goes back to her sketchbook like she never looked at all.
This is easy. Weirdly, scarily easy.
Hey — quick hi, gotta make the rounds, very busy, very important new-kid business.
Ha— okay, Quick's got somewhere to be. Respect the hustle.
Player swings by the window table next.
Hey — I saw the raccoon thing from across the room. Deeply upsetting. Ten out of ten.
It's supposed to be upsetting. That's the whole point.
Player finally lands in the open seats between both worlds, tray settling, pulse still a little quick.
Okay. Nobody yelled at me for that. I'll take it.
Okay, okay, okay — settle in, folks, we've got groups to make and not a lot of period left.
I'm pairing you up for the semester project, so get comfortable with whoever you land next to.
What if we mapped the school like it's got, I don't know, ecosystems? Cafeteria's the savanna, library's basically hibernation—
That's — actually really strong, Jordan. Hold that thought.
But the moment doesn't hold. Ms. Alvarez reads off the pairing list, and Ben's name lands next to player's.
Sorry — sorry, I'll just, whatever you want to do is fine, I don't want to be the reason we tank the grade—
lines.action: already talking fast, not looking up

lines.text: Man, tough break, Riley. Try not to catch whatever Ben's got.
A couple friends near Jordan laugh. A couple others throw him an uneasy glance before looking back down at their notes.

lines.action: goes very still, staring at his desk

lines.text: Honestly? Glad it's Ben. Pretty sure he's the only one who's said something smart all period.
Ben's shoulders drop half an inch.
...Guess I just ruin grades slower with company, then.
It's the first joke he's cracked all day. Dry. Unprompted. Entirely his own.
Jordan's eyebrows lift — caught off guard, like the moment didn't land how he meant it to.
...Whatever, man. Group project, not a group hug.
He doesn't apologize. He also doesn't push it further.
Small thing. Felt like a big thing.
Hey. So — what were you thinking for the project?
I mean — sorry, is that — I don't know, does that work? I was thinking the map doesn't have to be of a place, it could be of a—
—a feeling? Like the legend's emotional weather instead of, like, actual weather. Sorry, that's probably—
No, that's — actually really good.
Ben blinks, like he wasn't expecting to be taken seriously, then relaxes half an inch into his chair.
...Oh. Okay. Cool. I mean — cool, yeah, let's do that.
Didn't say anything to Jordan. Still feels like I did something.
Ha— yeah, no, I mean—
It feels fine for exactly as long as it takes to say it.
Ben glances over. Player catches the exact second his face falls — the half-smile gone before it even finished forming.
...Right. Cool. So — the project—
That laugh is gonna sit weird in my stomach for a while.
Ben, why don't you kick us off — I liked where your group's notes were headed.
Ben stands half out of his seat, papers rustling too loud in his hands.
So — okay, so the idea is, um, the map isn't — it's not really a place, it's more like—
He loses the thread. Jordan's table snickers, loud enough for the room to hear it.
Ms. Alvarez looks up from the board — a beat longer than a glance, taking the whole room in.
She turns back to her writing without a word. The silence that follows sits heavier than anything she could've said.
The snickering fades on its own. But Ben's still red. Still stuck mid-sentence. The room's attention lingering a beat too long.
It's a map of feelings, basically. The legend's emotional weather instead of actual weather. It's — honestly the best idea I've heard all period.
The laughter dies out fast. The room's attention shifts to player instead.
Riley, thank you for that — that's exactly the kind of building-on-an-idea I want to see more of in here.
A couple of Jordan's friends clock player differently now. Not unkindly. Just... noted.
Okay. That might cost something later. Didn't feel like it in the moment, though.
Player stays quiet. Ben mumbles through the rest of his sentence alone, and the moment passes the way bad moments do — not fixed, just over.
Later, packing up, player leans over.
Hey — that emotional weather thing? That's really good. Like, genuinely.
...Oh. You — really?
Yeah. We should lead with that.
Okay. Yeah. Let's — yeah.
Didn't say it when it counted. Said it anyway. Not sure which part matters more.
Honestly, my whole contribution was 'cafeteria equals danger zone,' so really, Ben's carrying this entire project.
The laughter shifts — softer now, aimed at player instead. Ben gets a second to breathe.
...Thanks.
Not brave. But not nothing, either.
The day's winding down. Backpacks, slammed lockers, the particular chaos of everyone trying to leave at once.
Hey — we're all heading to the place by the field, if you want in. No pressure. Just, you know. Should probably be there.
Maya lingers near the steps, sketchbook under her arm, clearly deciding whether to say something.
Ben walks past alone, glances back once, then keeps walking like he didn't.
Through the open door of Ms. Alvarez's classroom, player catches her jotting something in her roster next to Ben's name — quick, private, like she noticed more today than she let on.
Okay. Last one. Who are you gonna be tomorrow.
Player catches up with Ben and Maya at the bottom of the steps.
—okay so I was thinking, if we actually build the emotional weather thing out, we could do like a legend with actual symbols, like a little rain cloud for 'overwhelmed' and—
lines.action: already talking, a little too fast

lines.text: He's been like this since the bell rang.
Sorry — sorry, is that too much, I can—
No, keep going. I want to hear it.
Maya turns her sketchbook around — properly, this time. Real work. Not a joke drawing.
...Since you're apparently sticking around.
Smaller than the other option. Maybe a little more effort. Feels like starting something, not just joining it.
Player falls in with Jordan's group, and it's easy — the good kind of easy, laughter already going before player even finishes the story.
See, this is why I like you — you don't get weird about stuff. Come on, we're gonna be late.
Walking away, player notices Ben and Maya leaving in different directions, not together, not really looking back.
That's fine. Probably fine. Everyone splits off eventually.
Player tells Jordan's group they'll catch up later, then falls into step with Ben and Maya instead, at least for now.
Your loss, Quick. We'll save you a seat anyway.
Numbers get traded. Half-plans get made. Nothing's fully decided.
So — same time tomorrow? For the project, I mean. Or just — in general, either works.
...Both. Obviously.
Nothing's settled. That's almost the best part.

options.text: Laugh it off and introduce yourself with a steady handshake.
Quietly pick up your books and keep moving.
Fire back a joke of your own.

a.text: Riley. And hey — if this is the welcome wagon, I'd hate to see the goodbye one.

b.action: crouching, gathering books, not making eye contact

b.text: Jordan's already half-turned away, talking again before player finishes the sentence.

c.text: Relax, I've survived worse. Mostly gravity.

options.text: Sit with Maya, the girl alone by the window - she's not waiting to be invited, but there's an open seat across from her.
Join Jordan's table, already loud with easy laughter, waving you over like you're already part of the joke.
Say a quick hello at both tables, then land somewhere among the scattered open seats in between.

b.text: Quick! Or — Riley, was it — get over here, seat's basically already yours.

c.text: I'll just... say hi to everyone. That's a normal, sustainable plan.

options.text: Say, loud enough for the room, that you're glad to be paired with him.
Don't call it out, but immediately start working with Ben one-on-one.

a.text: Honestly? Lucky me. Ben's the one person here I've actually heard say something smart all period.

b.text: So — what were you thinking for the project?

options.text: Speak up and back Ben's idea in front of everyone.
Stay quiet now, then help Ben privately afterward.
Crack a joke about your own idea to pull attention away from Ben.

a.text: It's a map of feelings, basically. The legend's emotional weather instead of actual weather.

b.text: Not now. Later. That's still doing something, right?

c.text: Honestly, my whole contribution was 'cafeteria equals danger zone,' so really, Ben's carrying this.

options.text: Catch up with Ben and Maya instead.
Tell Jordan's group you'll catch up later, then walk partway with Ben and Maya.

a.action: peeling off toward Ben and Maya

c.text: Catch up with you guys later?
c.action: to Jordan, already turning toward Ben and Maya

lines.text: You've kind of been... the same person all day. Is that weird to say?
It's not weird. It's just rare.
I don't know. Felt easier than pretending, actually.
Yeah. That tracks.
...Didn't peg you for a window-table person. No offense.
Yeah, I mean — it's cool you're here. Just — kind of came out of nowhere, is all.
Yeah. I know. I'm — figuring some stuff out, I guess.
Okay. We'll see.
lines.action: studying player a beat too long

lines.text: You've been kind of everywhere today. In a good way! I just — wasn't sure you'd actually show up here.
Showing up's the whole thing, though. So.
I wanted to. This wasn't the leftover option.
...Noted.
Told you — you get it. Not everybody gets it, first day.
Guess I got lucky.
Nah. Instinct. That's different.
This is the version where nothing costs anything. So far.
Didn't think you'd actually pick us, ngl. Cool. Whatever, glad you did.
Weird. After today, this feels less like a win and more like... a different question.
Wonder what Ben's doing right now.
Knew you'd come around eventually, Quick.
Feels good. Also feels like the easy answer. Not sure those are the same thing.
Tomorrow, probably back to the window table. Or not. We'll see.
You know you can't do this every day, right? Eventually you have to pick a table.
Maybe. Not today, though.
...Fair enough.
I vote you keep doing this, for the record. Statistically it's working out.
You don't have to, you know. Split yourself in half for us.
I'm not — I just don't want to pick yet.
...Okay. Just don't disappear on us for the popular option.
Careful. Keep doing this and people start expecting you at both tables.
I mean — I'd take it. Any of you, honestly.
Don't know if I'm building something or just stalling. Either way, feels better than choosing wrong.

voiceNotes: Riley (player) is written to shift register based on scene partner: looser and quicker with Jordan, more careful/measured with Maya, gentler and more direct with Ben, and almost formal-polite with Ms. Alvarez — this code-switching is a deliberate through-line, not inconsistency. INTERNAL lines exist to surface the gap between what Riley says and what Riley means; they should almost always contradict, complicate, or undercut the line right before them rather than simply restate it. Jordan never apologizes directly — his accountability, when it appears (6a), shows up as a shrug and a subject change, never words. Maya's warmth is rationed and always hedged ('not because I have to,' 'don't,' 'we'll see') — when she drops the hedge entirely, it should feel earned and rare. Ben's fluency is a tell: watch his stammer disappear the instant he's talking about the actual idea (6b, 8a) — that shift IS the characterization, no need to flag it. Ms. Alvarez should almost never editorialize about Ben directly; her care is procedural and quiet (the beat of silence in scene-7, the private note in scene-8) — let the silence carry weight rather than explaining it.

alternatives: Whoa — okay, new kid's got hands. I respect it. Didn't even flinch.
Okay, that's a whole main-character entrance right there. I respect it.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Both lean on Jordan's established hype-man vocabulary; swap depending on whether the collision reads as more physical (books flying, near-fall) or more about composure, to keep the beat flexible for animation/staging.

alternatives: But you're not annoying. Yet. Sit if you want.
You can sit. Don't make it weird.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Maya's guardedness can be dialed up or down depending on how warm the studio wants this first-lunch beat to land; the 'yet' version keeps more armor on, the 'don't make it weird' version is drier and slightly more approachable.

alternatives: It's a map of feelings — the legend's emotional weather instead of actual weather. Ben's idea. It's the best one in the room.
Ben was saying it's a map of feelings, not places — I think that's exactly right.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original line risks sounding like Riley is claiming the idea as their own; these alternatives make the credit-to-Ben more explicit, which better serves the 'trust earned' beat and gives Ben clearer ownership in front of the class.

alternatives: Didn't think you'd actually pick us, ngl. Cool. Whatever, glad you did.
Oh — hey. Wasn't expecting you over here, honestly. Cool though.
alternativeLines.reasoning: First version leans into Jordan's deflect-with-casualness pattern; second is slightly more transparent about his surprise, which could read as more emotionally legible if the studio wants Jordan's anxiety-about-relevance trait more visible in this rare branch.
